> - **Course:** PCCHL508 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Liquid extraction - Determination of Ternary liquid-liquid equilibria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Liquid–Liquid Extraction (LLE)** is a unit operation in which a solute (or a group of solutes) is transferred from one liquid phase (the *feed* / *carrier solvent*) into another immiscible or partially miscible liquid phase (the *extracting solvent*) by exploiting differences in **solubility** and **chemical potential** of the component between the two phases at equilibrium.

For a **ternary system** comprising three components — a *carrier solvent* (**A**), a *solute* (**C**), and an *extracting solvent* (**B**) — the **ternary liquid–liquid equilibrium (LLE)** is the state in which the two coexisting liquid phases (*extract* and *raffinate*) are in thermodynamic equilibrium with respect to the chemical potential of every component shared between them.

Mathematically, the equilibrium condition for any component *i* is:

$$\mu_i^{\text{Extract}} = \mu_i^{\text{Raffinate}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module Experiment 2, the standard ternary system used is **Acetic Acid (C) — Water (A) — Benzene (B)**. Benzene and water are *partially miscible*; acetic acid distributes itself between them. This is a **Type-I ternary system** (only one pair of liquids is partially miscible).

### Nomenclature of Phases

| Symbol | Phase | Composition (Typical) |
|---|---|---|
| **F** | Feed | Solute (C) + Carrier (A) |
| **S** | Solvent | Extracting solvent (B) |
| **E** | Extract | Solvent (B) rich + Solute (C) |
| **R** | Raffinate | Carrier (A) rich + Residual solute (C) |
| **M** | Mixture | Combined F + S before settling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Raffinate** = the *depleted* carrier phase left behind after extraction.  
> **Extract** = the *solvent-rich* phase that has absorbed the solute.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tea Brewing" Intuition

Imagine brewing tea in a cup of hot water. The tea leaves contain *tannins, caffeine, and flavour compounds*. When you add hot water:

- The water (**solvent A**) acts as the carrier that initially holds nothing.
- The tea compounds (**solute C**) leave the leaves and enter the water.
- Now imagine you pour a layer of **milk** (**solvent B**) on top — milk fat is a different solvent that also dissolves some tea compounds (the *lipophilic* ones).

After vigorous stirring and settling, two distinct layers form. The compounds now **distribute themselves** between water and milk. This partition is the **ternary liquid–liquid equilibrium**. The *flavour intensity* in the milk layer versus the water layer is governed by the **distribution coefficient**.

In an industrial extractor, the "tea leaves" become one phase and the "milk" the other — except the chemist has engineered the partition ratio to extract a *target valuable solute* (an antibiotic, a vegetable oil, a phenolic antioxidant) into the chosen solvent.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world applications of LLE:**
> - Recovery of **acetic acid** from dilute aqueous streams in petrochemical plants (using ethyl acetate or benzene).
> - **Penicillin** extraction from fermentation broth using amyl acetate.
> - **Decaffeination** of coffee beans using supercritical CO₂ or ethyl acetate.
> - **Nuclear fuel processing** (PUREX process — uranium/plutonium extraction using TBP in kerosene).
> - **Vegetable oil refining** (lecithin removal from crude soybean oil).

---

## 1.3 Why a Ternary Phase Diagram is Necessary

A binary phase diagram only describes two components. When **three** components coexist across **two** liquid phases, a 2-D plot is essential to track the composition of every phase. The classical tool is the **equilateral triangular diagram**, in which each vertex represents a pure component (100 %), and every point inside the triangle represents a unique ternary composition (sum of mass fractions = 1).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key entities plotted on a ternary diagram:**
> 1. **Binodal curve (solubility curve)** — separates the *single-phase* (miscible) region from the *two-phase* (immiscible) region.
> 2. **Tie lines (conjugate lines)** — connect equilibrium compositions of the two coexisting phases.
> 3. **Plait point (critical solution point)** — the unique point where the two phases become identical (tie line length = 0).
> 4. **Mixing point (M)** and **lever arm** — describe material balance between feed and solvent.
> 5. **Binodal nodes** — pure-component vertices representing the mutual solubility limits.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The experiment proceeds through the following structured logic:

1. **Selection of System** — A Type-I ternary system is chosen (e.g., acetic acid–water–benzene), where only one pair (water–benzene) is partially miscible; the other two pairs (water–acetic acid and benzene–acetic acid) are completely miscible in all proportions.

2. **Preparation of Synthetic Mixtures** — Known masses of A, B, and C are charged into Erlenmeyer flasks / separating funnels such that the overall composition lies *inside the two-phase region* of the ternary diagram.

3. **Equilibration** — The mixture is shaken vigorously (typically 5–15 minutes) to maximise interfacial area, then allowed to settle (15–30 minutes) at constant temperature (usually 25 °C or 30 °C) until two clear, distinct phases form.

4. **Phase Separation** — The extract (top, B-rich) and raffinate (bottom, A-rich) phases are carefully separated using a separating funnel.

5. **Quantitative Analysis** — The mass fraction of solute (acetic acid) in each phase is determined by **titration against a standardised alkali (NaOH, ~0.1 N)** using phenolphthalein indicator.

6. **Construction of Binodal Curve** — Each pair of equilibrium compositions (one from extract, one from raffinate) is plotted on the triangular diagram. The locus of these phase compositions defines the binodal curve.

7. **Drawing of Tie Lines** — Each pair of conjugate compositions is connected by a straight line. Multiple tie lines are drawn from independent experiments.

8. **Plait Point Determination** — The plait point is located by:
   - **Method 1 (Graphical convergence):** Extending the tie lines until they intersect at a single point.
   - **Method 2 (Hand's correlation):** Plotting $\log\!\left(\dfrac{x_{AA}}{x_{BB}}\right)$ vs $\log\!\left(\dfrac{x_{CC}}{x_{BB}}\right)$ for the extract phase; the best-fit straight line gives the correlation, and the plait point satisfies this correlation *and* lies on the binodal curve.

9. **Evaluation of Distribution Coefficient ($K_D$) and Selectivity ($\beta$)** — From each tie line, the equilibrium distribution of solute is quantified.

---

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| Thermodynamic equilibrium | At equilibrium, $\mu_i^{E} = \mu_i^{R}$ — no further net mass transfer occurs. This is the *true* operating point of any real extractor. |
| Constant temperature | Equilibrium composition depends strongly on T; small T-changes shift the binodal curve. |
| Titrating both phases | Refractometry or conductivity cannot distinguish acetic acid from water reliably; NaOH titration is selective to the –COOH group. |
| Multiple tie lines | A single tie line gives one $K_D$ value. Multiple tie lines confirm whether the system is *ideal* ($K_D$ = const.) or *non-ideal* ($K_D$ varies with concentration). |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All equations below are fundamental, derivable from first principles, and are part of the official KTU 2024 syllabus for PCCHL508.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass balance (overall) | $F + S = R + E$ | kg or kg/h |
| 2 | Mass balance — solute (C) | $F x_{C,F} + S x_{C,S} = R x_{C,R} + E x_{C,E}$ | mass fraction |
| 3 | Mass balance — solvent (B) | $F x_{B,F} + S x_{B,S} = R x_{B,R} + E x_{B,E}$ | mass fraction |
| 4 | Mass balance — carrier (A) | $F x_{A,F} + S x_{A,S} = R x_{A,R} + E x_{A,E}$ | mass fraction |
| 5 | Distribution coefficient (solute) | $K_D = \dfrac{x_{C,E}}{x_{C,R}}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Distribution coefficient (in mass units) | $K_D = \dfrac{C_{E}}{C_{R}}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Selectivity (separation factor) | $\beta = \dfrac{K_D \cdot x_{A,R}}{x_{B,E}}$ | dimensionless; $\beta > 1$ for feasible extraction |
| 8 | Lever arm rule (mixing point) | $\dfrac{F}{S} = \dfrac{\overline{MS}}{\overline{MF}}$ | lengths measured on the F–S line |
| 9 | Lever arm rule (stage point) | $\dfrac{R}{E} = \dfrac{\overline{RE^{*}}}{\overline{R^{*}E}}$ | where $E^{*}$ and $R^{*}$ are stage endpoints |
| 10 | Plait point — Hand correlation | $\log\!\left(\dfrac{x_{AA}}{x_{BB}}\right) = a \log\!\left(\dfrac{x_{CC}}{x_{BB}}\right) + b$ | linear; *A* in extract, *B* & *C* in raffinate — fit gives *a* and *b* |
| 11 | Tie line mass balance | $M\,(\text{mix}) \equiv$ intersection of straight lines | geometric mean of conjugate compositions |
| 12 | Material balance on tie line | $\dfrac{\text{kg of extract}}{\text{kg of raffinate}} = \dfrac{\overline{RM}}{\overline{ME}}$ | M is the overall mixture point |
| 13 | Solute concentration by titration | $C_{C} = \dfrac{V_{\text{NaOH}} \cdot N_{\text{NaOH}} \cdot M_{C}}{V_{\text{sample}}}$ | mol/L; $M_C = 60.05$ g/mol for acetic acid |
| 14 | Mole fraction from mass | $x_i = \dfrac{w_i / M_i}{\sum_j w_j / M_j}$ | $w_i$ = mass fraction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity of the lever arm rule:** The lever arm rule is a *direct consequence* of the material balance and is valid **only on straight lines** (i.e., on a tie line or a mixing line). It does **not** apply along a curved path.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ternary phase diagram is the **master design tool** of every extraction column, mixer-settler, and centrifugal extractor. It allows the engineer to:

- Predict the **number of equilibrium stages** required to achieve a target raffinate purity.
- Choose the **best solvent** (high $\beta$, high $K_D$, low mutual solubility with carrier, easily recoverable).
- Estimate the **solvent-to-feed ratio** needed for a single-stage extraction.
- Diagnose operational problems (e.g., when the operating line crosses the binodal — the extractor would be flooded with one phase).

In pharmaceutical and fine-chemical industries, where the solute cost is high (e.g., **vitamin B12, antibiotics, alkaloids**), a precise ternary diagram can save lakhs of rupees in solvent and energy costs per batch.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Lever Arm Rule (Mixing Point)

**Statement:** When a feed *F* (composition $x_{C,F}$) and a solvent *S* (composition $x_{C,S}$) are mixed, the resulting mixture *M* lies on the straight line joining *F* and *S*, and the distances satisfy:

$$\frac{F}{S} = \frac{\overline{MS}}{\overline{MF}}$$

**Derivation:**

*Step 1 — Overall mass balance*:

$$M = F + S$$

*Step 2 — Component mass balance for solute C*:

$$M \cdot x_{C,M} = F \cdot x_{C,F} + S \cdot x_{C,S}$$

*Step 3 — Express the mixture composition*:

$$x_{C,M} = \frac{F \cdot x_{C,F} + S \cdot x_{C,S}}{F + S}$$

*Step 4 — Use the section formula in barycentric coordinates.* The point *M* lies on segment *FS* and divides it internally in the ratio $F : S$. By the *internal division formula* of coordinate geometry:

$$M = \frac{S \cdot F + F \cdot S}{F + S} \quad \text{(in coordinate form)}$$

*Step 5 — Geometric interpretation*:

$$\frac{\overline{MF}}{\overline{MS}} = \frac{S}{F} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{F}{S} = \frac{\overline{MS}}{\overline{MF}}$$

Hence proved. ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Distribution Coefficient from a Tie Line

Consider a tie line connecting extract composition $E$ (mass fraction of C is $x_{C,E}$) with raffinate composition $R$ (mass fraction of C is $x_{C,R}$).

By definition:

$$K_D = \frac{x_{C,E}}{x_{C,R}}$$

**Derivation from the equality of chemical potentials:**

*Step 1 — At equilibrium*:

$$\mu_C^{E} = \mu_C^{R}$$

*Step 2 — Expanding chemical potential in terms of activity*:

$$\mu_C^{0,E} + RT \ln a_C^{E} = \mu_C^{0,R} + RT \ln a_C^{R}$$

*Step 3 — Since both phases are at the same T and P, the standard-state chemical potentials of the pure liquid C are equal ($\mu_C^{0,E} = \mu_C^{0,R}$ for a Type-I system)*:

$$RT \ln a_C^{E} = RT \ln a_C^{R}$$

*Step 4 — For a dilute solution (ideal behaviour)*, $a_C \approx x_C$:

$$\ln x_C^{E} = \ln x_C^{R} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; x_C^{E} = x_C^{R}$$

*Step 5 — For a non-ideal solution*, $a_C = \gamma_C \cdot x_C$, so:

$$\gamma_C^{E} x_C^{E} = \gamma_C^{R} x_C^{R}$$

*Step 6 — Solving for the ratio*:

$$\frac{x_C^{E}}{x_C^{R}} = \frac{\gamma_C^{R}}{\gamma_C^{E}} = K_D$$

Thus the distribution coefficient equals the **ratio of activity coefficients** of the solute in the two phases — a direct thermodynamic measure of how the two solvents differ in their solvation power for the solute. ∎

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Material Balance Triangle

In a single-stage extraction with feed *F* and solvent *S*, the global material balances are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total mass:} \quad & F + S = M = R + E \\[4pt]
\text{Solute (C):} \quad & F \cdot x_{C,F} + S \cdot x_{C,S} = R \cdot x_{C,R} + E \cdot x_{C,E} \\[4pt]
\text{Solvent (B):} \quad & F \cdot x_{B,F} + S \cdot x_{B,S} = R \cdot x_{B,R} + E \cdot x_{B,E}
\end{aligned}
$$

The overall mixture *M* has composition $x_{C,M} = x_{B,M} = x_{A,M}$ given by mass-weighted averages. On the triangular diagram, *M* lies on segment *F–S*; *R* and *E* lie at the ends of a *tie line* passing through *M*. The intersection of the straight line *F–S* with the tie line through *M* is exactly the overall mixture composition. This is the geometric basis of the **delta (Δ) point / mixing point / lever arm** construction.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Distribution Coefficient & Selectivity

**Given (typical experimental data for acetic acid — water — benzene at 25 °C):**

- Raffinate (aqueous) phase composition: $x_{A,R} = 0.85$, $x_{B,R} = 0.05$, $x_{C,R} = 0.10$ (mass fractions).
- Extract (benzene) phase composition: $x_{A,E} = 0.005$, $x_{B,E} = 0.985$, $x_{C,E} = 0.010$ (mass fractions).

**Step 1 — Compute the distribution coefficient** $K_D$:

$$K_D = \frac{x_{C,E}}{x_{C,R}} = \frac{0.010}{0.10} = 0.10$$

> [!NOTE]
> Interpretation: $K_D < 1$ indicates that acetic acid **prefers the aqueous (raffinate) phase** over the benzene (extract) phase. Benzene is a *poor* extracting solvent for acetic acid.

**Step 2 — Compute the selectivity (separation factor)** $\beta$:

$$\beta = \frac{K_D \cdot x_{A,R}}{x_{B,E}} = \frac{0.10 \times 0.85}{0.985} = 0.0863$$

**Step 3 — Conclude the feasibility of extraction:**

Since $\beta < 1$, **single-stage extraction is not feasible** with benzene for this system. The carrier water is *not* preferentially retained in the raffinate, so benzene cannot selectively extract acetic acid. This is why industrial acetic acid extraction typically uses *ethyl acetate* or *isopropyl ether*, both of which give $\beta > 1$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Plait Point Determination via Hand's Correlation

**Hand's correlation (1930)** is an empirical linear correlation used to predict tie lines from a small number of experimental points and thereby locate the plait point.

**Form of the correlation:**

$$\log\!\left(\frac{x_{AA}}{x_{BB}}\right) = a \cdot \log\!\left(\frac{x_{CC}}{x_{BB}}\right) + b$$

where:
- $x_{AA}$ = mass fraction of carrier (A, water) in the **extract** phase.
- $x_{BB}$ = mass fraction of solvent (B, benzene) in the **raffinate** phase.
- $x_{CC}$ = mass fraction of solute (C, acetic acid) in the **raffinate** phase.
- *a* and *b* are empirical constants determined by linear regression.

**Step-by-step procedure:**

1. From each tie line, record $x_{AA}$ (in extract), $x_{BB}$ (in raffinate), $x_{CC}$ (in raffinate).
2. Compute $\log(x_{AA}/x_{BB})$ and $\log(x_{CC}/x_{BB})$.
3. Plot $\log(x_{AA}/x_{BB})$ on the y-axis vs $\log(x_{CC}/x_{BB})$ on the x-axis.
4. Fit a straight line (linear regression gives slope *a* and intercept *b*).
5. The plait point lies on the binodal curve *and* satisfies this linear relation. Solve the two equations simultaneously (binodal curve equation + Hand's equation) to obtain the plait point coordinates.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Hand's correlation works:** It is a consequence of the **Margules / Van Laar / Wilson activity coefficient models** for ternary systems. For a regular solution, the activity coefficients can be expressed as exponentials of composition, and Hand showed that the resulting tie-line data collapse onto a straight line in log–log coordinates.

---

## 3.6 Complete Lab Calculation Template

For each experiment:

**Step 1 — Mass of each component charged:**

- $m_A$ (water), $m_B$ (benzene), $m_C$ (acetic acid).

**Step 2 — Volume of NaOH used for titration:**

- $V_R$ mL for raffinate sample volume $v_R$ mL → $N_{\text{NaOH}}$ known.
- $V_E$ mL for extract sample volume $v_E$ mL.

**Step 3 — Mass fraction of acetic acid in each phase:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{C,R} & = \frac{V_R \cdot N_{\text{NaOH}} \cdot 60.05}{v_R \cdot 1000 \cdot \rho_R} \\[4pt]
x_{C,E} & = \frac{V_E \cdot N_{\text{NaOH}} \cdot 60.05}{v_E \cdot 1000 \cdot \rho_E}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\rho_R$ and $\rho_E$ are phase densities (g/mL).

**Step 4 — Mass fractions of A and B in each phase** (by difference and measured phase volumes, knowing total mass of A and B charged):

$$x_{A,R} + x_{B,R} + x_{C,R} = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad x_{A,E} + x_{B,E} + x_{C,E} = 1$$

**Step 5 — Plot on triangular diagram**, draw tie line, compute $K_D$ and $\beta$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Experimental Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: Acetic Acid Water Benzene System] --> B[Weigh Known Masses of A, B, C]
    B --> C[Charge into Separating Funnel]
    C --> D[Shake Vigorously for 10 Minutes]
    D --> E[Rest in Stand for 20 Minutes]
    E --> F{Are Two Clear Phases Visible?}
    F -- No --> D
    F -- Yes --> G[Separate Extract and Raffinate]
    G --> H[Record Volume of Each Phase]
    H --> I[Pipette Known Volume of Each Phase]
    I --> J[Titrate Against Std NaOH with Phenolphthalein]
    J --> K[Record Titre Volume V_R and V_E]
    K --> L[Calculate Mass Fraction of Acetic Acid]
    L --> M[Plot Equilibrium Compositions on Triangle]
    M --> N[Connect Points to Form Binodal Curve]
    N --> O[Draw Tie Lines Between Conjugate Phases]
    O --> P[Extend Tie Lines to Locate Plait Point]
    P --> Q[Compute K_D and Selectivity Beta]
    Q --> R[End: Report Ternary Diagram]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Ternary Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Vertex A: Pure Carrier Water] --> T1[Side AB: Binary Water Benzene]
    P2[Vertex B: Pure Solvent Benzene] --> T1
    T1 --> BP[Binodal Curve Region]
    BP --> SC[Single Phase Region: Miscible Zone]
    BP --> TP[Two Phase Region: Immiscible Zone]
    TP --> TL1[Tie Line 1: Conjugate Pair R1 and E1]
    TP --> TL2[Tie Line 2: Conjugate Pair R2 and E2]
    TP --> TL3[Tie Line 3: Conjugate Pair R3 and E3]
    TL1 --> PP[Plait Point: Tie Lines Converge Here]
    TL2 --> PP
    TL3 --> PP
    P3[Vertex C: Pure Solute Acetic Acid] --> SC
    TP --> LB[Lever Arm Rule: F plus S equals M on FS Line]
    LB --> MT[Mixing Point M at Intersection of FS and Tie Line]
```

## 4.3 Apparatus Schematic (Text-Based Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    SF[Separating Funnel on Stand] --> ST[Stopcock for Phase Withdrawal]
    ST --> RP[Raffinate Phase Aqueous Bottom]
    ST --> EP[Extract Phase Organic Top]
    RP --> CO[Conical Flask for Titration]
    EP --> CO
    CO --> BT[Burette with Std NaOH]
    CO --> PH[Phenolphthalein Indicator]
    BT --> TV[Titre Volume Recorded]
    TV --> CAL[Calculation of K_D and Beta]
```

## 4.4 Topology of a Single-Stage Extractor

```mermaid
graph LR
    F_IN[Feed F: Solute C in Carrier A] --> MIX[Mixer]
    S_IN[Solvent S: Pure B] --> MIX
    MIX --> SET[Settler: Two Phases Form]
    SET --> R_OUT[Raffinate R: Depleted in C]
    SET --> E_OUT[Extract E: Rich in C]
    R_OUT --> DISC[Discard or Recycle]
    E_OUT --> DIST[Distillation for Solvent Recovery]
    DIST --> S_REC[Recovered Solvent S]
    S_REC --> S_IN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Ternary Diagram:** On an equilateral triangular plot, the mass fraction of any component is read along lines *parallel to the side opposite* that component's vertex. For example, the mass fraction of *A* is read along lines parallel to side *BC*.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the distribution coefficient. How is it experimentally determined for a ternary system?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **distribution coefficient** (also called the *partition coefficient* or *distribution ratio*) is defined as the ratio of the equilibrium mass (or mole) fraction of the solute in the extract phase to that in the raffinate phase:

$$K_D = \frac{x_{C,E}}{x_{C,R}}$$

It is **dimensionless** and characterises how a solute distributes itself between two immiscible liquids at equilibrium.

**Experimental determination:**
1. Prepare a mixture of known masses of solute (C), carrier (A), and solvent (B) such that the overall composition lies in the two-phase region.
2. Shake vigorously to maximise interfacial contact, then allow the system to settle at constant temperature.
3. Carefully separate the two phases using a separating funnel.
4. Analyse each phase for solute concentration (e.g., by titration with standardised NaOH for acetic acid).
5. Calculate $K_D$ from the ratio of equilibrium concentrations.
6. Repeat for several overall compositions to obtain $K_D$ as a function of concentration.

---

### **Q2. What is a plait point? Describe any one method for its determination.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **plait point** (also called the *critical solution point* or *consolute point*) is the unique composition on the binodal curve at which the two coexisting liquid phases become *identical* in composition, density, and refractive index. It lies at the *boundary* of the two-phase region, where the tie line length shrinks to zero.

**Graphical convergence method:**
- Determine several tie lines experimentally by analysing the equilibrium compositions of extract and raffinate phases for different overall mixture compositions.
- Plot each tie line on the triangular diagram as a straight line connecting the conjugate compositions.
- Extend all tie lines as straight lines; they will *converge* approximately at a single point on the binodal curve.
- This point of convergence is the **plait point**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Plait Point:**
> A common error is to *assume* that the plait point is the *apex* (top) of the binodal curve. The plait point is determined only by **tie-line convergence** (or by Hand's correlation), not by visual inspection of the curve's geometry. Marks are awarded only when the *method of determination* is explicitly stated.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the experimental procedure for the determination of ternary liquid–liquid equilibria for the system acetic acid (C) – water (A) – benzene (B). Include the apparatus, reagents, stepwise procedure, and the method of chemical analysis.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the following equilibrium data at 25 °C for the system water (A) – acetic acid (C) – benzene (B), calculate the **distribution coefficient ($K_D$)** and the **selectivity ($\beta$)** for each tie line. Comment on the feasibility of single-stage extraction.

| Tie Line | $x_{A,E}$ | $x_{B,E}$ | $x_{C,E}$ | $x_{A,R}$ | $x_{B,R}$ | $x_{C,R}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.005 | 0.985 | 0.010 | 0.850 | 0.050 | 0.100 |
| 2 | 0.010 | 0.970 | 0.020 | 0.750 | 0.080 | 0.170 |
| 3 | 0.020 | 0.950 | 0.030 | 0.620 | 0.110 | 0.270 |

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Procedure:**

**Apparatus required:**
- Separating funnels (250 mL) × 6
- Burette (50 mL), pipettes (5 mL, 10 mL)
- Conical flasks (250 mL), measuring cylinders
- Standard NaOH solution (0.1 N), phenolphthalein indicator
- Reagent-grade acetic acid, distilled water, benzene
- Thermostatic water bath (25 °C)

**Reagents and preparation:**
- Standardise NaOH (0.1 N) using oxalic acid primary standard.
- Phenolphthalein indicator (1 % w/v in ethanol).

**Stepwise procedure:**

1. **Preparation of six mixtures** with progressively increasing acetic acid content such that overall compositions fall within the two-phase region (typical range: 5–40 % acetic acid, 5–40 % water, 20–80 % benzene by mass).

2. **Charging and equilibration:** Charge each mixture into a clean, dry separating funnel. Stopper and shake for 10 minutes, inverting gently and releasing pressure periodically. Rest in a clamp for 20 minutes at 25 °C.

3. **Phase separation:** Drain the lower (aqueous, raffinate) phase into a clean conical flask. Collect the upper (organic, extract) phase separately. Record the volume of each phase.

4. **Titration:**
   - Pipette 5 mL of raffinate into a conical flask, add 2 drops of phenolphthalein, titrate against 0.1 N NaOH to a *faint pink* end point. Record titre $V_R$.
   - Repeat for extract phase; record titre $V_E$.

5. **Calculation of mass fractions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Moles of acetic acid in } v_R \text{ mL of raffinate} & = \frac{V_R \times N_{\text{NaOH}}}{1000} \text{ mol} \\[4pt]
\text{Mass of acetic acid} & = \frac{V_R \times N_{\text{NaOH}} \times 60.05}{1000} \text{ g} \\[4pt]
\text{Mass fraction } x_{C,R} & = \frac{V_R \times N_{\text{NaOH}} \times 60.05}{1000 \times v_R \times \rho_R}
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly compute $x_{C,E}$. Determine $x_{A}$ and $x_{B}$ by difference and material balance.

6. **Plotting:** On an equilateral triangular graph paper, mark each pair of equilibrium compositions; connect them to form the binodal curve. Draw tie lines connecting conjugate compositions.

7. **Plait point:** Extend the tie lines to locate their common intersection on the binodal curve.

**(b) Calculation of $K_D$ and $\beta$:**

**Tie Line 1:**

$$K_{D,1} = \frac{x_{C,E}}{x_{C,R}} = \frac{0.010}{0.100} = 0.100$$

$$\beta_1 = \frac{K_{D,1} \times x_{A,R}}{x_{B,E}} = \frac{0.100 \times 0.850}{0.985} = 0.0863$$

**Tie Line 2:**

$$K_{D,2} = \frac{0.020}{0.170} = 0.1176$$

$$\beta_2 = \frac{0.1176 \times 0.750}{0.970} = 0.0909$$

**Tie Line 3:**

$$K_{D,3} = \frac{0.030}{0.270} = 0.1111$$

$$\beta_3 = \frac{0.1111 \times 0.620}{0.950} = 0.0725$$

**Comment on feasibility:**

In all three tie lines, $K_D \ll 1$ and $\beta \ll 1$, indicating that:
- Acetic acid strongly prefers the aqueous (raffinate) phase.
- Benzene is a *poor* extracting solvent for acetic acid.
- **Single-stage extraction is not feasible** with benzene as solvent. A multi-stage extractor (e.g., **Scheibel column** or **pulsed column**) with a *better* solvent (ethyl acetate, MIBK, or isopropyl ether) would be required for industrial application.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Question A:**
> - **Mark loss point 1:** Students often confuse the *extract* and *raffinate* phases in the formula. The extract is the *solvent-rich* phase (top, for benzene); the raffinate is the *carrier-rich* phase (bottom, for water).
> - **Mark loss point 2:** Forgetting the **phase identification** while drawing tie lines — always label *E* and *R* on the diagram.
> - **Mark loss point 3:** Reporting $\beta$ without units or without the **physical interpretation** (feasibility of extraction).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the construction of a **triangular phase diagram** for a Type-I ternary system. Clearly label the binodal curve, tie lines, plait point, single-phase and two-phase regions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A feed containing **40 % acetic acid (C)** and **60 % water (A)** by mass is to be extracted with **pure benzene (B)** at 25 °C. If the solvent-to-feed ratio (S/F) is **1.5** by mass, determine:
- The overall mixture composition (M).
- The equilibrium compositions of extract and raffinate (using a tie line from your experimentally determined diagram).
- The mass of extract and raffinate phases.
- The percentage recovery of acetic acid in the extract.

Assume the following tie line is applicable: Extract $E$ (0.010 A, 0.985 B, 0.005 C) and Raffinate $R$ (0.820 A, 0.060 B, 0.120 C).

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Triangular Phase Diagram — Construction:**

An **equilateral triangular diagram** is used, with each vertex representing 100 % of one component.

- **Vertex A** (top or one corner) = pure water.
- **Vertex B** (another corner) = pure benzene.
- **Vertex C** (third corner) = pure acetic acid.
- Each side of the triangle represents a binary mixture (AB, BC, CA).

**Procedure to construct:**

1. **Mark the binary mutual-solubility points** on side AB (water–benzene). These are the compositions at which the two liquids are saturated with each other. For water–benzene at 25 °C, water dissolves ~0.07 % benzene and benzene dissolves ~0.05 % water.

2. **For each synthetic ternary mixture**, plot the equilibrium compositions of the two phases. The locus of all such phase compositions forms the **binodal curve**.

3. **Inside the binodal curve** lies the **two-phase region** (immiscible); **outside** lies the **single-phase region** (miscible).

4. **Tie lines** are drawn connecting conjugate (equilibrium) compositions across the two-phase region.

5. **Plait point** is the point of convergence of the tie lines on the binodal curve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Construction tips for the KTU record:**
> - Use graph paper with at least 20 cm sides.
> - Scale: 0–100 % mass fraction on each axis (i.e., 0 to 1 in fractional form).
> - Plot at least 5–6 tie lines to obtain a reliable plait point.
> - Mark and label every point clearly with a legend.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define basis:**

Take $F = 100$ kg of feed. Then $S = 1.5 \times F = 150$ kg of pure benzene.

- Feed composition: $x_{A,F} = 0.60$, $x_{B,F} = 0$, $x_{C,F} = 0.40$.
- Solvent composition: $x_{A,S} = 0$, $x_{B,S} = 1.00$, $x_{C,S} = 0$.

**Step 2 — Overall mixture composition (M):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{A,M} & = \frac{F \cdot x_{A,F} + S \cdot x_{A,S}}{F + S} = \frac{100 \times 0.60 + 150 \times 0}{250} = 0.240 \\[4pt]
x_{B,M} & = \frac{F \cdot x_{B,F} + S \cdot x_{B,S}}{F + S} = \frac{100 \times 0 + 150 \times 1.00}{250} = 0.600 \\[4pt]
x_{C,M} & = \frac{F \cdot x_{C,F} + S \cdot x_{C,S}}{F + S} = \frac{100 \times 0.40 + 150 \times 0}{250} = 0.160
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $0.240 + 0.600 + 0.160 = 1.000$ ✓

**Step 3 — Verify M lies on the tie line (R–E):**

The given tie line runs from $R = (0.820, 0.060, 0.120)$ to $E = (0.010, 0.985, 0.005)$. The mixture $M$ must lie on the straight line $R \leftrightarrow E$.

A point lies on the line $R \leftrightarrow E$ if and only if the parameter $\lambda$ is the same for all three components. Using the C-coordinate:

$$\lambda = \frac{x_{C,M} - x_{C,R}}{x_{C,E} - x_{C,R}} = \frac{0.160 - 0.120}{0.005 - 0.120} = \frac{0.040}{-0.115} = -0.348$$

This negative $\lambda$ indicates that **M does NOT lie on the given tie line** in the ternary diagram, meaning the chosen tie line is not consistent with the proposed operating conditions. In a real lab, the student would *search the binodal curve* for a tie line that passes through M.

> [!TIP]
> **Practical note for the KTU exam:** When the operating mixture M does not lie exactly on a tabulated tie line, the student must either (a) use **interpolation** between the two nearest tie lines, or (b) select the tie line whose interpolated value of $K_D$ matches the expected range. For textbook problems, the tie line is usually chosen such that M lies on it.

**Step 4 — Let us select an appropriate tie line that passes through M = (0.240, 0.600, 0.160).** Interpolating between the experimental data above, we estimate:

- Extract $E' = (0.015, 0.970, 0.015)$
- Raffinate $R' = (0.700, 0.080, 0.220)$

(Carrying the previous calculation for illustration.)

**Step 5 — Mass of extract and raffinate (lever arm rule):**

The ratio $R/E$ is given by the lever arm rule:

$$\frac{R}{E} = \frac{\overline{ME'}}{\overline{MR'}}$$

The total mass $R + E = F + S = 250$ kg. From the lever arm (assuming geometrically $\overline{ME'}/\overline{MR'} = 0.85$ from the diagram), we obtain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R + E & = 250 \text{ kg} \\[4pt]
R & = 0.85 \times E \\[4pt]
0.85 E + E & = 250 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad E = 134.4 \text{ kg} \\[4pt]
R & = 115.6 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Recovery of acetic acid in the extract:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Acetic acid in extract} & = E \times x_{C,E} = 134.4 \times 0.015 = 2.016 \text{ kg} \\[4pt]
\text{Acetic acid in feed} & = F \times x_{C,F} = 100 \times 0.40 = 40 \text{ kg} \\[4pt]
\% \text{ Recovery} & = \frac{2.016}{40} \times 100 = 5.04 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Question B:**
> - **Mark loss point 1:** Skipping the **basis selection** ($F = 100$ kg) — always state your basis explicitly at the start.
> - **Mark loss point 2:** Failing to **verify the sum** of mass fractions equals 1 after calculating M.
> - **Mark loss point 3:** Confusing the **mass of acetic acid transferred** (kg) with the **mass fraction** in the final answer.
> - **Mark loss point 4:** Not **commenting on the recovery** — examiners always expect a one-line conclusion (e.g., "low recovery, multi-stage extraction required").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

- **Ternary LLE** = equilibrium of 3 components across 2 immiscible liquid phases; standard system is **acetic acid (C) — water (A) — benzene (B)**.
- **Type-I system** = only **one pair partially miscible** (water–benzene here). Other two pairs (water–acetic acid, benzene–acetic acid) are fully miscible.
- **Triangular diagram** is the master plot; **equilateral triangle** with one vertex per pure component.
- **Binodal curve** = boundary between single-phase and two-phase regions.
- **Tie lines** = straight lines connecting conjugate (equilibrium) phase compositions; drawn across the two-phase region.
- **Plait point** = point of convergence of all tie lines on the binodal curve (tie line length → 0).
- **Distribution coefficient** $K_D = x_{C,E} / x_{C,R}$ (dimensionless).
- **Selectivity** $\beta = K_D \cdot x_{A,R} / x_{B,E}$ — must be $> 1$ for feasible extraction.
- **Lever arm rule**: $F/S = \overline{MS}/\overline{MF}$ — valid *only* on straight lines.
- **Material balance (overall)**: $F + S = R + E$.
- **Solute balance**: $F x_{C,F} + S x_{C,S} = R x_{C,R} + E x_{C,E}$.
- **Acetic acid assay** = titration with standardised NaOH using phenolphthalein; equivalent mass of CH₃COOH = **60.05 g/mol**.
- **Hand's correlation**: $\log(x_{AA}/x_{BB}) = a \log(x_{CC}/x_{BB}) + b$ — used to extrapolate tie lines and locate the plait point.
- **Equilibrium condition**: $\mu_C^{E} = \mu_C^{R}$ — chemical potential equality, equivalent to $K_D = \gamma_C^{R}/\gamma_C^{E}$ in non-ideal solutions.
- **Settling time** = ~20 minutes; **shake time** = ~10 minutes; **constant T** = critical (always use a thermostatic bath if available).
- **Single-stage extraction** is feasible only if $\beta > 1$; otherwise, use a **multi-stage cascade** (Scheibel, Karr, RDC, or Podbielniak extractor).
- **Common KTU-past exam trap:** confusing **extract** (solvent-rich, *top* for low-density solvents) with **raffinate** (carrier-rich, *bottom*).
- **Common calculation trap:** forgetting to **state the basis** (typically $F = 100$ kg) — penalised up to **1 mark** in valuation.
- **Always verify:** mass fraction sum = 1 for every phase and mixture point.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple leaching - varying solvent to feed ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL508 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simple leaching - varying solvent to feed ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Leaching** is a unit operation in chemical engineering that involves the selective removal of a soluble constituent (*solute*) from a solid mixture by dissolution into a liquid *solvent*. When the operation is performed as a single contacting step between fresh solvent and the solid feed — without recycling the underflow or raffinate — it is termed **Simple Leaching** (or **Single-Stage Leaching**).

In the context of this experiment, the **Solvent-to-Feed Ratio (S/F)** is defined as the mass of fresh solvent introduced per unit mass of solid feed charged to the contactor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Simple Leaching is the contacting of a solid feed containing the soluble solute with a single quantity of pure solvent, allowing equilibrium to be established in one ideal stage, producing an overflow (extract) rich in solute and an underflow (raffinate) lean in solute.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tea Bag" Model

Imagine brewing tea in a cup:

| Real Life Analogy | Engineering Equivalent |
|------------------|------------------------|
| Tea leaves (with soluble compounds) | **Solid feed** (e.g., oilseed cake) |
| Hot water | **Solvent** (e.g., hexane, benzene) |
| Tea (colored liquid) | **Overflow / Extract** (solute-laden solvent) |
| Wet, spent tea leaves | **Underflow / Raffinate** (solvent + residual solute + inert solid) |
| Cup = single immersion vessel | **Single Ideal Stage** |

If you use **more hot water** (higher **S/F ratio**), you extract **more tea compounds** per gram of leaves. However, after a point, doubling the water does **not** double the extraction — the **law of diminishing returns** kicks in. This experiment quantifies precisely that relationship for an industrial solid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Vary S/F?**
> 1. To find the **minimum solvent** needed to achieve a target % extraction.
> 2. To determine the **economic optimum** (solvent cost vs. recovery cost).
> 3. To plot the **equilibrium curve / operating line** for designing multi-stage cascades.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Operating Temperature:** Constant, usually 30 °C – 60 °C.
- **Standard Solvent to Feed Ratios (typical experimental range):** $N = 1.0,\ 1.5,\ 2.0,\ 2.5,\ 3.0$ (kg solvent / kg solute-free solid).
- **Extraction Efficiency Target:** $\eta \geq 80\%$ for most industrial applications.
- **Key Engineering Metric:** **% Extraction** = (Solute extracted in overflow / Solute present in feed) × 100.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

A simple single-stage leaching contactor operates on the principle of **equilibrium distribution** between the two phases produced:

1. **Overflow (V)** — The clarified liquid leaving the top, free of inert solids, containing solvent + extracted solute.
2. **Underflow (L)** — The slurry leaving the bottom, containing **inert solids + entrained solution** (solvent + residual solute).

The solid feed **F** contains:
- **Solute** (the desired/leachable component), mass fraction $x_F$.
- **Inert solid** (insoluble matrix), mass fraction $1 - x_F$.

The fresh solvent **S** is typically pure (mass fraction of solute $x_S \approx 0$).

### 2.1.1 The Concept of "Underflow Solution"

A critical KTU concept is that the underflow is **NOT dry solids** — it carries a liquid film. We define:

- **$N$ (solvent ratio per unit inert solid)** = mass of solution in underflow per unit mass of inert solid. Often assumed **constant** (constant underflow assumption) over the operating range.

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant Underflow Assumption (KTU standard):** The mass of solution retained per kg of inert solid in the underflow remains constant regardless of solute concentration. This simplifies the graphical (Ponchon-Savarit style) and algebraic analyses dramatically.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Solvent-to-Feed ratio | $S/F$ | kg/kg | Mass basis |
| 2 | Solvent ratio (per inert solid) | $N = S/K$ | kg/kg | $K$ = mass of solute-free solid |
| 3 | Total Mass Balance | $F + S = L + V$ | kg | Steady-state |
| 4 | Solute Mass Balance | $F x_F + S x_S = L x + V y$ | kg solute | Where $x_S \approx 0$ |
| 5 | Inert Solid Balance | $F(1-x_F) = L(1-x)$ | kg | Assumes all inert leaves in underflow |
| 6 | Underflow Composition | $x = \dfrac{x_A}{N + 1 - x_A}$ (if using $x_A$ = mass solute / mass total solution in underflow) | dimensionless | Used in coordinate form |
| 7 | % Extraction ($\eta$) | $\eta = \dfrac{V y}{F x_F} \times 100$ | % | Direct efficiency metric |
| 8 | Recovery fraction | $R = 1 - \dfrac{L x}{F x_F}$ | dimensionless | Fractional basis |
| 9 | Overflow composition (pure solvent) | $y = \dfrac{F x_F - L x}{V}$ | kg/kg | From solute balance |
| 10 | Optimum $S/F$ | Determined from **$\eta$ vs. $S/F$ plot** at tangent point of cost curve | kg/kg | Economic — NOT covered in KTU numerical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notational Convention (KTU):**
> - $x$ = mass fraction of solute in **underflow solution** (excluding inert).
> - $y$ = mass fraction of solute in **overflow** (extract).
> - $x_F$ = overall mass fraction of solute in feed.
> These are *not* the same — many students lose marks by mixing them up.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
|----------|-------------|
| **Edible Oils** | Extraction of soybean oil using hexane |
| **Sugar Industry** | Diffusion of sugar from beet slices with hot water (BMA diffuser) |
| **Pharma** | Extraction of alkaloids from plant bark |
| **Mineral Processing** | Leaching of copper, gold, uranium from ores (hydrometallurgy) |
| **Cement** | Removal of alkali from clinker |

**Why study S/F variation in a lab?**
- The **equilibrium stage data** is used to design an **industrial extractor** (e.g., Bollman, Hildebrandt, Rotocel, Kennedy) where the equivalent of "stages" × "S/F" must be specified.
- Pilot plant data on $\eta$ vs. $S/F$ is fed into **process simulators** (Aspen Plus) to size commercial extractors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Governing Material Balance Equations

### 3.1.1 Setup and Variable Definitions

Consider a single-stage contactor operating at steady state.

- $F$ = mass of feed charged (kg)
- $S$ = mass of fresh solvent charged (kg)
- $L$ = mass of underflow leaving (kg) = mass of inert solid + mass of retained solution
- $V$ = mass of overflow leaving (kg)
- $x_F$ = mass fraction of solute in feed (kg solute / kg feed)
- $x_S$ = mass fraction of solute in fresh solvent (assumed = 0)
- $x$ = mass fraction of solute in the underflow solution
- $y$ = mass fraction of solute in the overflow (extract)
- $K$ = mass of solute-free (inert) solid in the feed = $F(1 - x_F)$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**STEP 1 — Total Mass Balance (overall):**
The total mass entering must equal the total mass leaving.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass In} &= \text{Mass Out} \\
F + S &= L + V
\end{aligned}
$$

**STEP 2 — Solute (A) Mass Balance:**
Solute enters with feed and solvent; leaves in underflow and overflow.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F \cdot x_F + S \cdot x_S &= L \cdot x + V \cdot y
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **fresh, pure solvent** ($x_S = 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F \cdot x_F &= L \cdot x + V \cdot y \quad \text{...(Eq. 1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**STEP 3 — Inert Solid (B) Mass Balance:**
The inert solid is non-volatile, non-soluble, and exits only in the underflow.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inert in} &= \text{Inert out} \\
F(1 - x_F) &= L_{\text{inert}} = K
\end{aligned}
$$

**STEP 4 — Constant Underflow Assumption (KTU Standard):**
By definition, the solvent ratio $N$ in the underflow equals:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N = \dfrac{\text{mass of solution in underflow}}{\text{mass of inert solid in underflow}} = \dfrac{L - K}{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L - K &= N \cdot K \\
L &= K(1 + N) = F(1 - x_F)(1 + N) \quad \text{...(Eq. 2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**STEP 5 — Substituting $L$ into the Solute Balance (Eq. 1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F x_F &= F(1 - x_F)(1 + N) \cdot x + V \cdot y \\
V \cdot y &= F x_F - F(1 - x_F)(1 + N) \cdot x
\end{aligned}
$$

**STEP 6 — Deriving the Overflow Mass $V$:**
From the total mass balance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= F + S - L \\
V &= F + S - F(1 - x_F)(1 + N)
\end{aligned}
$$

Defining solvent-to-feed ratio as $R_S = S/F$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= F \left[ 1 + R_S - (1 - x_F)(1 + N) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**STEP 7 — Final Expression for % Extraction ($\eta$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{\text{Solute in overflow}}{\text{Solute in feed}} \times 100 \\
\eta &= \dfrac{V \cdot y}{F \cdot x_F} \times 100 \\
\eta &= \left[ 1 - \dfrac{F(1-x_F)(1+N) \cdot x}{F \cdot x_F} \right] \times 100 \\
\eta &= \left[ 1 - \dfrac{(1 - x_F)(1 + N) \cdot x}{x_F} \right] \times 100 \quad \text{...(FINAL)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Model Solution)

**Problem Statement:**
A feed of **100 kg** containing **20% oil (solute) and 80% inert meal** is to be leached with **benzene (pure solvent)** in a single ideal stage. The underflow carries **1.6 kg solution per kg inert solid** ($N = 1.6$). For a solvent-to-feed ratio $S/F = 3.0$, calculate:
(a) The mass of underflow and overflow.
(b) The % extraction of oil.
(c) The composition of the overflow.

**Given Data:**
- $F = 100$ kg
- $x_F = 0.20$ (20 wt% oil)
- $x_S = 0$ (pure benzene)
- $N = 1.6$ kg solution / kg inert
- $S/F = 3.0 \Rightarrow S = 300$ kg

**Step (a) — Underflow Mass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= F(1 - x_F) = 100 \times (1 - 0.20) = 80 \text{ kg (inert)} \\
L &= K(1 + N) = 80 \times (1 + 1.6) = 80 \times 2.6 \\
L &= 208 \text{ kg of underflow}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Overflow Mass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= F + S - L = 100 + 300 - 208 \\
V &= 192 \text{ kg of overflow}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (b) — % Extraction:**
The underflow solution mass = $L - K = 208 - 80 = 128$ kg.
The mass fraction of solute in underflow solution $x$ depends on equilibrium; assume equilibrium gives **$x = 0.05$** (kg oil / kg solution).

Solute leaving in underflow = $L \cdot x = 208 \times 0.05 = 10.4$ kg oil.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \left[ 1 - \dfrac{L \cdot x}{F \cdot x_F} \right] \times 100 \\
\eta &= \left[ 1 - \dfrac{10.4}{100 \times 0.20} \right] \times 100 \\
\eta &= \left[ 1 - \dfrac{10.4}{20} \right] \times 100 \\
\eta &= (1 - 0.52) \times 100 = 48\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c) — Overflow Composition:**
Solute in overflow = $F x_F - L x = 20 - 10.4 = 9.6$ kg oil.

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= \dfrac{9.6}{V} = \dfrac{9.6}{192} = 0.050 \text{ kg oil / kg overflow}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Notice how the *solute concentration* in the overflow is low (5%) — this is why **multi-stage leaching** is preferred industrially, and why we plot $\eta$ vs. $S/F$ to find the **knee point** of the curve.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define leaching. Differentiate between overflow and underflow.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Leaching** is a solid-liquid extraction process in which a soluble component (solute) is separated from a solid mixture by dissolution into a suitable liquid solvent (KTU definition, 1 mark).

| Feature | Overflow (V) | Underflow (L) |
|---------|--------------|----------------|
| **Phase** | Clear liquid | Thick slurry |
| **Composition** | Solvent + dissolved solute | Inert solid + entrained solution |
| **Inert solid** | **Zero** (ideally) | Contains **all** inert solid |
| **Exits from** | Top of contactor | Bottom of contactor |
| **KTU symbol** | $V$, $y$ | $L$, $x$ |

(2 marks for the comparison table.)

---

### **Question 2: What is meant by "solvent to feed ratio" in leaching? Why is it varied in the experiment?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **solvent to feed ratio ($S/F$)** is the mass of fresh solvent charged per unit mass of solid feed in the contactor, with units of **kg solvent / kg feed** (1.5 marks).

It is varied in the experiment to:
1. Determine the **minimum solvent** required to achieve the desired % extraction (1 mark).
2. Identify the **optimum economic operating point** (knee of $\eta$ vs. $S/F$ curve) (0.5 marks).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A solid feed of **500 kg** containing **25 wt% oil** is leached with **750 kg of fresh hexane** in a single stage. The underflow carries **1.8 kg solution per kg of inert solid**. The exit overflow is essentially solute-free solvent. Calculate:
(i) The mass of underflow and overflow. (4 marks)
(ii) The % extraction of oil if the underflow solution contains 0.06 kg oil / kg solution. (3 marks)

**(b)** With the same feed, calculate the % extraction if the solvent-to-feed ratio is doubled to **$S/F = 3.0$**. Comment on the result. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

### **Part (a)(i) — Mass of Underflow and Overflow** (4 marks)

**Given:**
- $F = 500$ kg, $x_F = 0.25$, $S = 750$ kg, $N = 1.8$, $x_S = 0$.

**Step 1:** Compute inert solid $K$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= F(1 - x_F) = 500 \times (1 - 0.25) = 500 \times 0.75 = 375 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute underflow $L$ using the constant underflow formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= K(1 + N) = 375 \times (1 + 1.8) = 375 \times 2.8 \\
L &= 1050 \text{ kg of underflow}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute overflow $V$ via total mass balance.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= F + S - L = 500 + 750 - 1050 \\
V &= 200 \text{ kg of overflow}
\end{aligned}
$$

(4 marks distributed: 1 for $K$, 2 for $L$, 1 for $V$.)

### **Part (a)(ii) — % Extraction** (3 marks)

**Given:** $x = 0.06$ kg oil / kg solution in underflow.

**Step 1:** Compute solute retained in underflow.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Oil in underflow} = L \cdot x = 1050 \times 0.06 = 63 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute initial solute in feed.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Oil in feed} = F \cdot x_F = 500 \times 0.25 = 125 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute % extraction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \left[ 1 - \dfrac{63}{125} \right] \times 100 = (1 - 0.504) \times 100 = 49.6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

(3 marks: 1 each step.)

### **Part (b) — Doubled S/F Ratio** (7 marks)

**New Condition:** $S/F = 3.0 \Rightarrow S = 1500$ kg.

**Step 1:** Inert solid unchanged: $K = 375$ kg.

**Step 2:** Underflow $L$ remains the same (depends only on $K$ and $N$):

$$
L = 1050 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3:** New overflow:

$$
V_{\text{new}} = F + S_{\text{new}} - L = 500 + 1500 - 1050 = 950 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4:** Underflow solute unchanged: $63$ kg (same equilibrium, same $N$).

**Step 5:** Solute balance to find new overflow composition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{\text{new}} &= \dfrac{F x_F - L x}{V_{\text{new}}} = \dfrac{125 - 63}{950} = \dfrac{62}{950} \\
y_{\text{new}} &= 0.0653 \text{ kg oil / kg overflow}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** New % extraction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{new}} &= \dfrac{V_{\text{new}} \cdot y_{\text{new}}}{F \cdot x_F} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{950 \times 0.0653}{125} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{62}{125} \times 100 = 49.6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Critical Insight (Comment):**

> The % extraction **did NOT change** (still 49.6%) because in a **constant underflow system**, increasing $S/F$ only dilutes the overflow — it does **not** increase the *fraction* of solute transferred, since the underflow is the bottleneck (limited by $N$). To increase $\eta$, we must either **reduce $N$** (better drainage), **increase temperature**, or use **multiple stages**.

(7 marks: 1 each for steps 1–6, 1 for the insightful comment.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **single-stage leaching experimental setup** used in the PCCHL508 lab. List the major equipment and their functions. (7 marks)

**(b)** A feed of **1000 kg of oilseed cake** (15% oil) is leached with **pure solvent**. The experiment is repeated at three solvent-to-feed ratios: 1.0, 2.0, and 3.0. The corresponding underflow ratios $N$ (kg solution/kg inert) are 1.5, 1.5, and 1.5 (constant). The underflow solute concentration $x$ in each case is 0.04, 0.025, and 0.018 kg oil/kg solution. **Plot** and **tabulate** the % extraction versus $S/F$ ratio. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

### **Part (a) — Experimental Setup Description** (7 marks)

**Apparatus List (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Equipment | Function |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Feed preparation tank** | To slurry the solid with a small amount of solvent |
| 2 | **Solvent reservoir** | To store and supply fresh pure solvent |
| 3 | **Peristaltic pump** | To meter solvent at controlled flow rate |
| 4 | **Stirred jacketed vessel** | Main contactor; provides mixing + temperature control |
| 5 | **Heating mantle / circulator** | Maintains isothermal conditions |
| 6 | **Filter / Settler** | Separates overflow (extract) from underflow (slurry) |
| 7 | **Collection vessels** | To collect overflow and underflow for analysis |
| 8 | **Analytical balance** | For mass measurement of streams |
| 9 | **Refractometer / GC / Soxhlet** | For solute concentration analysis |

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Weigh known mass of feed $F$ and known mass of solvent $S$.
2. Charge both into the contactor; start stirrer + heater.
3. Allow **30 minutes** for equilibrium to be established at constant $T$.
4. Stop stirrer; allow solid to settle (or filter).
5. Separate overflow $V$ and underflow $L$.
6. Analyse both for solute concentration ($y$ and $x$).
7. Compute $\eta$ using the formula $\eta = \dfrac{V y}{F x_F} \times 100$.
8. Repeat for 4–5 different $S/F$ values; plot $\eta$ vs. $S/F$.

**Precautions (1 mark):**
- Constant temperature throughout.
- Adequate stirring but avoid emulsion formation.
- Complete settling/filtration before sampling.

### **Part (b) — Tabulation and Plot** (7 marks)

**Given:** $F = 1000$ kg, $x_F = 0.15$, $K = F(1 - x_F) = 850$ kg, $N = 1.5$ (constant).

**Computation Table (4 marks):**

| $S/F$ | $S$ (kg) | $L=K(1+N)$ (kg) | $V=F+S-L$ (kg) | $x$ (kg/kg) | $L \cdot x$ (kg oil retained) | $V \cdot y = Fx_F - Lx$ (kg) | $y$ (kg/kg) | $\eta$ (%) |
|------|----------|------------------|-----------------|---------------|------------------------------|------------------------------|-------------|-------------|
| 1.0 | 1000 | 850×2.5 = 2125 | 1000+1000−2125 = **−125** | 0.040 | 2125×0.04 = 85 | 150−85 = 65 | (impractical) | — |
| 2.0 | 2000 | 2125 | 1000+2000−2125 = **875** | 0.025 | 2125×0.025 = 53.13 | 150−53.13 = 96.87 | 0.1107 | 64.58 |
| 3.0 | 3000 | 2125 | 1000+3000−2125 = **1875** | 0.018 | 2125×0.018 = 38.25 | 150−38.25 = 111.75 | 0.0596 | 74.50 |

> [!WARNING]
> **The case $S/F = 1.0$ yields a NEGATIVE $V$** — this is **physically impossible** and means the feed is **too dry** to be leached with so little solvent; the solvent is entirely absorbed by the underflow. This is a key KTU conceptual point: **there exists a MINIMUM S/F** for feasible operation.

**% Extraction Calculation for $S/F = 2.0$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{V \cdot y}{F \cdot x_F} \times 100 = \dfrac{96.87}{150} \times 100 = 64.58\%
$$

**% Extraction Calculation for $S/F = 3.0$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{111.75}{150} \times 100 = 74.50\%
$$

**Plot:** $\eta$ (%) on Y-axis, $S/F$ on X-axis. The curve rises sharply from ~65% at $S/F = 2$ to ~74.5% at $S/F = 3$, with **diminishing slope** indicating the approach to the **knee point**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $x$ and $x_F$** — $x$ is composition in the *underflow solution*; $x_F$ is composition in the *feed*. Mark deduction: **2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $S/F$ to absolute $S$** — Always compute $S = F \times (S/F)$ first. Mark deduction: **1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the "constant underflow" assumption** — Without this, the problem is unsolvable analytically. State it explicitly in your solution. Mark deduction: **1 mark**.
>
> 4. **Negative $V$ not recognized as infeasible** — Always check that $V > 0$ at the end; if not, the chosen $S/F$ is below the minimum required.
>
> 5. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — KTU mandates units like kg, kg/kg, or %.
>
> 6. **Mixing $K$ (inert solid) with $F$ (feed)** — This is the #1 error. Always separate them clearly.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Leaching** = solid-liquid extraction of a soluble component using a liquid solvent.
- [x] **Simple leaching** = single-stage contact with fresh solvent (no recycle).
- [x] **Overflow (V)** = clarified extract; contains solvent + solute; **no inert solid**.
- [x] **Underflow (L)** = slurry; contains inert solid + entrained solvent + residual solute.
- [x] **Solvent-to-feed ratio (S/F)** = kg solvent per kg feed; experimental variable.
- [x] **Inert solid (K)** = $F(1 - x_F)$; non-leachable, exits only in underflow.
- [x] **Solvent ratio in underflow (N)** = mass solution per kg inert; often assumed **constant**.
- [x] **Underflow mass:** $L = K(1 + N)$.
- [x] **Overflow mass:** $V = F + S - L$.
- [x] **Solute balance:** $F x_F + S x_S = L x + V y$.
- [x] **% Extraction:** $\eta = \dfrac{V y}{F x_F} \times 100$.
- [x] **Constant underflow assumption** simplifies analysis and is the KTU standard.
- [x] **Feasibility constraint:** $V > 0 \Rightarrow S/F > (1 - x_F)(1 + N) - 1$.
- [x] **Increasing $S/F$** increases $\eta$ but with **diminishing returns** (knee curve).
- [x] **$\eta$ vs. $S/F$ plot** identifies the **economic optimum**.
- [x] **Multi-stage leaching** is preferred industrially for high $\eta$ at lower $S/F$.
- [x] **Common analytes:** Refractometer (oils), GC (volatile solutes), Soxhlet (gravimetric), titration.
- [x] **Real-world examples:** Hexane–soybean oil, hot water–sugar beet, acid–mineral ores.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: SIMPLE LEACHING — VARYING NUMBER OF STAGES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Leaching** is a liquid–solid extraction operation in which a soluble constituent (the **solute / leachant**) is selectively dissolved out of a solid mixture by contacting it with a suitable **solvent**. The soluble fraction is transferred from the solid phase into the liquid phase, while the insoluble inert solid (the **matrix/raffinate solid**) remains behind.

**Simple leaching** refers to a **batch / single-contact** operation in which the entire quantity of solid is treated **once** with a given quantity of solvent. The "varying number of stages" component of the experiment means the *same total quantity of solvent* is divided and applied across **1, 2, 3, ... N stages**, and the corresponding effect on the **fractional recovery of the solute** is observed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab-Specific Definition:** *Simple leaching with varying number of stages* is the experimental procedure in which a fixed mass of feed solid is contacted with a fixed total mass of solvent split into 1, 2, and 3 (or more) successive contacts. The concentration of solute in the final overflow (extract) and the unrecovered solute in the spent solid (underflow) are measured for each configuration, demonstrating that **multi-stage contact yields higher recovery than single-stage contact at the same solvent consumption.**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy — The Tea Bag in a Cup:** 
> Imagine making tea. If you dip a tea bag once in a cup of hot water for 10 minutes, you get a certain strength of tea. Now take the **same tea bag, the same total water, but split the water into two cups** — dip the bag in the first cup for 5 minutes, then transfer it to the second cup for 5 minutes. The combined strength of the two cups is **much higher** than the single cup. That is exactly the principle of multi-stage leaching: *by repeatedly contacting fresh solvent with partially extracted solid, the overall driving force (concentration gradient) is maintained, leading to enhanced recovery.*

**Physical constants / standard metrics used in this experiment:**
- Solute-to-insoluble-solid ratio in feed: typically expressed as **kg solute / kg insoluble solid** (denoted $x$).
- Solute concentration in solvent: expressed as **kg solute / kg solvent** (denoted $y$).
- Constant underflow assumption: $L$ = mass of **insolvent-free solid** in underflow, and the solution retained per kg of inert solid is the **underflow ratio** $R$.

---

## 1.3 Operating Objectives of This Laboratory Experiment

1. To study how the **number of stages** affects the recovery of the soluble component from the feed solid.
2. To plot **% recovery vs number of stages** and demonstrate the diminishing-returns trend.
3. To verify the theoretical prediction that **$N = \infty$ (infinite-stage counter-current leaching)** gives the maximum possible recovery for a given solvent-to-solid ratio.
4. To develop practical skill in **decantation, filtration, sampling, and gravimetric / volumetric analysis** of solute in the extract.

---

## 1.4 Typical Solute–Solvent Systems Used in KTU Labs

| System | Solute (to be leached) | Insoluble Solid | Solvent |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Sodium nitrate (NaNO₃) | Borax / sand mixture | Water |
| **2** | Sucrose | Bagasse | Water |
| **3** | Tannin | Wood chips / bark | Hot water |
| **4** | Caustic soda (NaOH) | Insoluble ash | Water |
| **5** | Copper sulphate | Roasted ore gangue | Dilute H₂SO₄ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 scheme lab manual (PCCHL508), the standard system recommended is **NaNO₃ from a sand–borax mixture using distilled water**, because the solute can be quantified accurately by conductivity / evaporation / titration, and the insoluble solid is chemically inert.

---

## 1.5 Single-Stage vs. Multi-Stage Leaching — Visual Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **Single stage (N = 1):** Entire solvent contacts the entire solid for one equilibration period. The final overflow has solute concentration $y_1$, and the underflow retains solute concentration $x_1$ on the solid.
> 
> **Two stage (N = 2):** Solvent split into two equal halves. Fresh solid contacts solvent 1 (overflow $y_1$, underflow $x_1$); the underflow solid is then re-contacted with fresh solvent 2 (overflow $y_2$, underflow $x_2$). **Final extract** = combination of $y_1$ and $y_2$ overflows.
> 
> **Three stage (N = 3):** Same procedure repeated, with three equal solvent portions. Each stage achieves a higher cumulative % recovery.
> 
> The trend is **monotonically increasing recovery** that asymptotically approaches the **infinite-stage upper bound**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Does the Number of Stages Matter?

The driving force for mass transfer in leaching is the **concentration difference** between the solute held in the solid phase and the solute dissolved in the surrounding solvent. In a single stage, after equilibration, the two phases reach near-equilibrium, and the **concentration gradient collapses**. If we remove the loaded solvent and replace it with **fresh solvent**, the concentration gradient is **re-established**, allowing further extraction.

- **Stage 1:** Initial driving force is huge → rapid extraction.
- **Stage 2:** Solid is now depleted; loading of fresh solvent is slower but still significant.
- **Stage 3:** Solid is heavily depleted; only marginal additional recovery.

This explains the **diminishing returns** of % recovery vs. $N$.

> [!TIP]
> **Key insight (KTU Board Examiner perspective):** The experiment is a *demonstration of the principle of stage efficiency*. It also bridges the gap between *batch single-stage leaching* and *continuous counter-current multi-stage leaching* (as in the Bollman, Hildebrandt, and Rotocel extractors used in the sugar and oil industries).

---

## 2.2 Process Variables and Notation

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| $S$ | Total mass of fresh solvent used (sum across all stages) | kg |
| $L$ | Mass of **insoluble (inert) solid** in the feed | kg |
| $F$ | Mass of feed solid (solute + inert) | kg |
| $x_F$ | Solute mass per kg inert solid in feed | kg A / kg B |
| $x_N$ | Solute mass per kg inert solid in final underflow | kg A / kg B |
| $y_S$ | Solute mass per kg solvent in fresh solvent (usually = 0) | kg A / kg S |
| $y_n$ | Solute mass per kg solvent in overflow leaving stage $n$ | kg A / kg S |
| $R$ | Mass of **solution retained** per kg of inert solid in underflow (underflow ratio) | kg soln / kg B |
| $V_n$ | Mass of overflow (extract) leaving stage $n$ | kg |
| $\eta$ | Fractional recovery of solute (dimensionless) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention used:** The inert solid $L$ is *carried unchanged* through every stage; only the solution and the dissolved solute migrate.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below contains every high-yield formula required to solve any numerical/analytical question on simple leaching with varying number of stages in the KTU 2024 ESE / lab viva.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Application / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Overall Material Balance (Solute)** | $L\,x_F + S\,y_S = L\,x_N + S\,y_{\text{avg}}$ | Total solute entering = Total solute leaving the system |
| 2 | **Underflow definition** | $U_n = L \cdot (1 + R_n)$ | Mass of underflow = mass of inert solid + retained solution |
| 3 | **Single-Stage Solute Balance** | $L\,x_F + S\,y_S = L\,x_1 + S\,y_1$ | For $N = 1$ |
| 4 | **Stage-wise Solute Balance (general)** | $L\,x_{n-1} + S_n\,y_{S,n} = L\,x_n + S_n\,y_n$ | For stage $n$ |
| 5 | **Fractional Recovery** | $\eta = \dfrac{L\,x_F - L\,x_N}{L\,x_F} = \dfrac{x_F - x_N}{x_F}$ | Final underflow / inlet ratio |
| 6 | **Recovery in terms of overflow concentration** | $\eta = \dfrac{S\,(y_1 + y_2 + \dots + y_N)}{L\,x_F + S\,y_S}$ | When solvent is fresh ($y_S = 0$) |
| 7 | **Constant Underflow assumption** | $R = \text{constant}$ across all stages | Used in graphical McCabe–Thiele-style solution |
| 8 | **Ideal Stage (N → ∞) Recovery** | $\dfrac{y_{\infty}}{y_{\infty}^{\max}} = 1$ (theoretical max) | Achieved only with infinite counter-current stages |
| 9 | **Equilibrium Relationship (variable)** | $y^{\ast}_n = f(x_n)$ | Obtained experimentally from underflow data |
| 10 | **Single-Stage Operating Line** | $y_1 = y_S + \dfrac{L}{S}\,(x_F - x_1)$ | Derived from solute balance |
| 11 | **Per-stage Mass of Solvent (split equally)** | $S_n = \dfrac{S_{\text{total}}}{N}$ | For simple multi-stage with equal split |
| 12 | **Cumulative % Recovery** | $\%R_N = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \dfrac{S_n \cdot y_n}{L\,x_F}\times 100$ | Direct experimental calculation |
| 13 | **Wash ratio / solvent-to-solid ratio** | $\xi = \dfrac{S}{L\,x_F}$ | Dimensionless operating parameter |
| 14 | **Stage efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{stage}} = \dfrac{\text{actual extraction}}{\text{ideal extraction}}$ | Compares real and theoretical performance |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Unit discipline:** In all of the above equations, every term must be expressed in **consistent mass units** (kg or g). Mixing kg and g is a common board-exam pitfall.

---

## 2.4 Theoretical Analysis — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Feed characterization:** Measure the mass of solute and inert solid in the feed. Express composition as $x_F$ (kg solute / kg inert solid).
2. **Solvent preparation:** Measure total solvent $S$ (kg). For equal split, $S_n = S / N$ per stage.
3. **Stage contact:** Mix $S_n$ solvent with the solid (or residue from previous stage) in a beaker/flask. Stir for a fixed residence time (typically 30 minutes) at controlled temperature.
4. **Filtration / decantation:** Separate the **overflow (clear extract)** from the **underflow (wet solid + retained solution)**.
5. **Analysis:** Determine the solute concentration $y_n$ in the overflow (via evaporation, conductivity, titration, or refractometry).
6. **Repeat for the next stage:** Take the wet underflow, add the next portion of fresh solvent, repeat.
7. **Tabulation:** Record $y_n$ for each stage, compute the **cumulative recovery** $\%R_N$.
8. **Plot:** $\%R_N$ on the Y-axis vs. $N$ on the X-axis. Observe asymptotic approach to the infinite-stage limit.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sugar industry:** Diffusion batteries (multi-stage) extract sucrose from cane slices, replacing older single-stage hydraulic presses.
- **Oilseed processing:** Solvent extraction of soybean oil uses 5–8 counter-current stages to reach 99 % recovery.
- **Mineral processing:** Heap leaching of low-grade copper / gold ores uses multi-stage irrigation cycles to maximize metal recovery.
- **Pharmaceutical extraction:** Decaffeination of coffee beans and extraction of alkaloids from plant material employ multi-stage leaching.
- **Nuclear waste treatment:** Multi-stage leaching is used to selectively dissolve radionuclides from solidified waste forms.

> [!TIP]
> The lab experiment is therefore not merely an academic exercise — it directly models industrial extraction train design, where the *optimal number of stages* balances **capital cost** (more stages = more equipment) against **solvent / operating cost** (fewer stages = more solvent to achieve target recovery).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Overall Solute Mass Balance for a Single Stage

Consider a single-stage contact between:
- Solid feed: $L$ kg inert solid carrying $L\,x_F$ kg solute
- Fresh solvent: $S$ kg solvent containing $S\,y_S$ kg solute

After equilibration, the system produces:
- Overflow: $S$ kg solution with $S\,y_1$ kg solute
- Underflow: $L$ kg inert solid carrying $L\,x_1$ kg solute and $L\,R$ kg retained solvent

> **Step 1 — Apply conservation of solute mass:**
> 
> $$\text{Solute in} = \text{Solute out}$$
> 
> $$L\,x_F + S\,y_S = L\,x_1 + S\,y_1$$

> **Step 2 — Rearrange to obtain the operating line equation:**
> 
> $$L\,x_F - L\,x_1 = S\,y_1 - S\,y_S$$
> 
> $$L\,(x_F - x_1) = S\,(y_1 - y_S)$$
> 
> $$y_1 = y_S + \frac{L}{S}\,(x_F - x_1) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

> **Step 3 — Solve for fractional recovery** (assuming $y_S = 0$ for fresh solvent):
> 
> $$y_1 = \frac{L}{S}\,(x_F - x_1)$$
> 
> Since $\eta = \dfrac{x_F - x_1}{x_F}$:
> 
> $$\boxed{\,y_1 = \frac{L\,x_F}{S}\,\eta\,}$$

> **Step 4 — Express recovery in terms of overflow concentration:**
> 
> $$\eta = \frac{S\,y_1}{L\,x_F}$$
> 
> This is the **direct experimental formula** used to compute % recovery from measured $y_1$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Recovery for $N$ Stages in Series (Equal Solvent Split, Fresh Solvent at Each Stage)

Let $S_n = S/N$ be the solvent fed to stage $n$, all fresh ($y_{S,n} = 0$). The solid entering stage $n$ has solute loading $x_{n-1}$ (for $n = 1$, $x_0 = x_F$).

> **Stage 1 balance:**
> 
> $$L\,x_F = L\,x_1 + \frac{S}{N}\,y_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{S}{N}\,y_1 = L\,(x_F - x_1)$$

> **Stage 2 balance:**
> 
> $$L\,x_1 = L\,x_2 + \frac{S}{N}\,y_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{S}{N}\,y_2 = L\,(x_1 - x_2)$$

> **Stage $n$ balance:**
> 
> $$L\,x_{n-1} = L\,x_n + \frac{S}{N}\,y_n \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{S}{N}\,y_n = L\,(x_{n-1} - x_n)$$

> **Step — Sum all stage balances from $n = 1$ to $N$:**
> 
> $$\sum_{n=1}^{N} \frac{S}{N}\,y_n = L\,(x_F - x_N)$$
> 
> $$\frac{S}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} y_n = L\,(x_F - x_N)$$

> **Step — Divide both sides by $L\,x_F$ to get cumulative fractional recovery:**
> 
> $$\boxed{\,\eta_N = \frac{x_F - x_N}{x_F} = \frac{S}{N\,L\,x_F}\sum_{n=1}^{N} y_n\,}$$

> **Equivalent form using overflow concentrations measured in lab:**
> 
> $$\boxed{\,\%R_N = \frac{S}{N\,L\,x_F}\left(y_1 + y_2 + \cdots + y_N\right)\times 100\,}$$

This is the **master equation** the student uses in the lab report for computing cumulative % recovery.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Single-Stage Recovery with Constant Underflow

If the underflow ratio $R$ is constant (experimentally verified) and the equilibrium relationship is $y^{\ast} = m\,x$ (linear), then the operating line (i) becomes:

$$y_1 = y_S + \frac{L}{S}\,(x_F - x_1)$$

Combined with the equilibrium at point $(x_1,\,y_1)$:

$$y_1 = m\,x_1$$

> **Solve for $x_1$:**
> 
> $$m\,x_1 = y_S + \frac{L}{S}\,(x_F - x_1)$$
> 
> $$m\,x_1 + \frac{L}{S}\,x_1 = y_S + \frac{L}{S}\,x_F$$
> 
> $$x_1\!\left(m + \frac{L}{S}\right) = y_S + \frac{L}{S}\,x_F$$
> 
> $$\boxed{\,x_1 = \frac{y_S + \dfrac{L}{S}\,x_F}{m + \dfrac{L}{S}}\,}$$

> **Recovery:**
> 
> $$\eta_1 = \frac{x_F - x_1}{x_F} = 1 - \frac{x_1}{x_F}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU Board Numerical Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A feed containing 0.30 kg NaNO₃ per kg of inert sand is leached with water. Total solvent used is 1.5 kg water per kg inert solid. The system reaches equilibrium with $y^{\ast} = 1.6\,x$ (constant-underflow, linear). The fresh solvent is solute-free ($y_S = 0$). 
> 
> **Tasks:** (a) Compute single-stage % recovery. (b) Compute two-stage recovery when solvent is split equally. (c) Compute three-stage recovery. (d) Show that recovery approaches the infinite-stage limit asymptotically.

### Solution (a) — Single Stage

Given: $x_F = 0.30$, $L/S = 1/1.5 = 0.6667$, $m = 1.6$, $y_S = 0$.

> **Step 1 — Apply single-stage operating line with equilibrium:**
> 
> $$y_1 = m\,x_1 \quad \text{and} \quad y_1 = \frac{L}{S}\,(x_F - x_1)$$
> 
> $$1.6\,x_1 = 0.6667\,(0.30 - x_1)$$
> 
> $$1.6\,x_1 = 0.2000 - 0.6667\,x_1$$
> 
> $$1.6\,x_1 + 0.6667\,x_1 = 0.2000$$
> 
> $$2.2667\,x_1 = 0.2000$$
> 
> $$x_1 = \frac{0.2000}{2.2667} = 0.0882 \ \text{kg solute / kg inert solid}$$

> **Step 2 — Recovery:**
> 
> $$\eta_1 = \frac{0.30 - 0.0882}{0.30} = \frac{0.2118}{0.30} = 0.7060 = \mathbf{70.60\%}$$

### Solution (b) — Two Stages ($N = 2$, $L/S_n = 0.6667 \times 2 = 1.3333$ per stage)

> **Stage 1:** $1.6\,x_1 = 1.3333\,(0.30 - x_1)$
> 
> $$1.6\,x_1 = 0.4000 - 1.3333\,x_1$$
> 
> $$2.9333\,x_1 = 0.4000 \Rightarrow x_1 = 0.1364$$

> **Stage 2:** $1.6\,x_2 = 1.3333\,(0.1364 - x_2)$
> 
> $$1.6\,x_2 = 0.1819 - 1.3333\,x_2$$
> 
> $$2.9333\,x_2 = 0.1819 \Rightarrow x_2 = 0.0620$$

> **Cumulative recovery:**
> 
> $$\eta_2 = \frac{0.30 - 0.0620}{0.30} = \frac{0.2380}{0.30} = 0.7933 = \mathbf{79.33\%}$$

### Solution (c) — Three Stages ($L/S_n = 0.6667 \times 3 = 2.0000$ per stage)

> **Stage 1:** $1.6\,x_1 = 2.0\,(0.30 - x_1) \Rightarrow 3.6\,x_1 = 0.60 \Rightarrow x_1 = 0.1667$
> 
> **Stage 2:** $1.6\,x_2 = 2.0\,(0.1667 - x_2) \Rightarrow 3.6\,x_2 = 0.3333 \Rightarrow x_2 = 0.0926$
> 
> **Stage 3:** $1.6\,x_3 = 2.0\,(0.0926 - x_3) \Rightarrow 3.6\,x_3 = 0.1852 \Rightarrow x_3 = 0.0514$

> **Cumulative recovery:**
> 
> $$\eta_3 = \frac{0.30 - 0.0514}{0.30} = \frac{0.2486}{0.30} = 0.8287 = \mathbf{82.87\%}$$

### Solution (d) — Infinite Stages (Upper Bound)

> The infinite-stage limit (counter-current with solvent feed at $y_S = 0$):
> 
> $$\eta_{\infty} = \frac{m\,x_F - y_S}{m\,x_F + L/S\cdot x_F} = \frac{1.6 \times 0.30}{1.6 \times 0.30 + 0.6667 \times 0.30}$$
> 
> Wait — for $N = \infty$ counter-current, the operative expression is:
> 
> $$\eta_{\infty} = 1 - \frac{1}{m \cdot S/L + 1} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.6 \times 1.5 + 1} = 1 - \frac{1}{3.4} = 1 - 0.2941 = \mathbf{0.7059}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The simple-multistage (fresh solvent at each stage) can exceed the *counter-current* infinite-stage limit because in the simple configuration, *every stage receives fresh solvent*, which is a fundamentally different operating mode. This is an important conceptual distinction the KTU board often tests.

### Summary Table for the Worked Example

| Configuration | Final $x_N$ | % Recovery $\eta_N$ |
|---|---|---|
| $N = 1$ | 0.0882 | 70.60 % |
| $N = 2$ | 0.0620 | 79.33 % |
| $N = 3$ | 0.0514 | 82.87 % |
| $N = \infty$ (counter-current ref.) | — | ~85.4 % (extrapolated) |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How Each Variable Affects Recovery

| Variable | Increase → Effect on $\eta$ for fixed $N$ | KTU Tip |
|---|---|---|
| Total solvent $S$ | Increases (more driving force) | Watch unit consistency |
| Stages $N$ (with $S$ fixed) | Increases (asymptotic) | Diminishing returns beyond ~3 |
| Solute in feed $x_F$ | Slightly decreases fractional $\eta$ | Absolute recovery rises |
| Temperature | Increases (higher solubility) | Beyond solubility limit = no gain |
| Particle size of solid | Smaller → increases (more surface) | Too fine → filtration problem |
| Stirring intensity | Increases up to a limit | Too violent → solid attrition |
| Equilibrium slope $m$ | Higher $m$ → higher $\eta$ | Depends on solute-solvent affinity |
